title: Mark Zuckerberg on Building the New Internet (ft. MrBeast) - YouTube
text:
People talk about wanting generational
change and leadership and culture and
this is it. Isn't the toothpaste kind of
out of a tube? I just don't think that a
lot of creators today think about
Facebook. I just want to harp on this
cuz I'm telling you it will change
everything. I I agree. What was your
experience watching that movie? It was
weird, man. If I'm going too hard, you
just cut it out. You make it seem like
I'm some kind of barbarian.
Today on the Colin Spir show, we're
joined by Facebook founder and Meta CEO
Mark Zuckerberg. I consider this to be
like the final boss of tech CEOs. We've
had a lot of tech CEOs on the channel in
the past year or two, but Mark
Zuckerberg is the final boss. So, at the
end of last year, Mark talked about this
return to OG Facebook, which the
Facebook that that we grew up with, this
more intimate, exclusive environment.
And the first move that he's making is
bringing back the friends tab, a place
on Facebook now where you can just
engage directly with your friends. So,
we wanted to talk to Mark about why make
this move now. What does this mean for
the future of how people connect on the
internet? And more specifically, what
does this mean for us as creators if the
internet moves in a direction of more
intimate connections? In this episode,
we are also joined by Mr. Beast. How's
it going? How's it going, man? Jimmy
gives some pretty candid thoughts about
Facebook and what he would do as the
CEO. All right, here's our conversation
with Mark Zuckerberg.
[Music]
Mark Zuckerberg, welcome to the show.
Thanks for having me. Good to see you
guys. Thanks for being with us, man. Um,
so you just announced that you're
bringing the friends tab back to
Facebook and that's like very
reminiscent to how we started using
Facebook. Um there was this time when
you launched the newsfeed where we went
from going, you know, just being in this
environment with just our friends and a
place to connect with our friends and,
you know, social media. The newsfeed
kind of laid the foundation for what
became the creator economy, like the
ability for content creators like us,
brands, publishers to create um pieces
of content that went out to many people,
millions of people. So, so why bring
back this concept of the friends tab
now? Why come back to the one to one or
so? This is this is phase one of of
bringing back OG Facebook. Okay. Um we
got All right. So, so here's here's
basically how I'm thinking about this.
You know, started Facebook and you know,
it started off as this online directory
for people to get to know the people
around them in their communities. We had
all these different utilities on on kind
of old school Facebook. And
then it sort of evolved in this
direction that one part of it which was
sharing and newsfeed just completely
kind of outscaled and um and ended up
being by far the most important part of
the product. And we just focused on that
and we focused on it and now we're here
21 years later and and that feed part in
the sharing ecosystem, you know, it's
used by three billion people around the
world. People spend a lot of time on it.
They enjoy it. But this funny thing
happened which is that a lot of the
other kind of fun and useful parts of uh
the original experience. Um we just sort
of didn't focus as much on. And not only
did we not focus on them as much, but I
I kind of look around the internet today
and I realize that no one else actually
recreated a lot of these things that
used to be pretty magical about Facebook
either. So, I actually think that
there's this whole opportunity that I
think is going to be pretty fun to to go
after and build, which is just to kind
of go one by one and build up a a bunch
of these things that used to be uh these
joyful experiences that that people had
as part of Facebook that just kind of
don't exist on the internet today. So, I
I think you know the the friends tab is
um is kind of an early and simple one,
right? It's kind of like newsfeed, but
it's just all the content from your
friends. Um and the idea is just to put
a little bit more of that emphasis uh
back on on connecting with with friends.
But I this is really phase one. We got a
lot more stuff like this coming. I'm
pretty excited about this. It's going to
be a fun project. So, you mentioned
there OG Facebook. I vividly remember
joining OG Facebook and today I actually
I filtered my account back to dates in
like 2006 2007 and on any given day I'm
basically using Facebook to like text my
friends right or like DM essentially
like it's just going nonstop but my
question to you with like adding the
friends tab back and trying to create
more friendto friend connections is
isn't the toothpaste kind of out of the
tube potentially in terms of users
now having experience with the feed and
wanting almost to rely on the algorithm.
Like I assume sometimes the algorithm
maybe knows what I want more so than my
friends do. Yeah. I mean I think for
general content and entertainment that's
probably true. You know, I don't think
it was a mistake that newsfeed ended up
being the main way that people use not
only Facebook, but basically all of the
other major feeds and and social
products that are out there, too. Um, I
think that that's this is like pretty
clearly a it's a it's a great product.
It's a great way to to kind of learn
about the world, to um to find content
that's going to be relevant to you, and
to have different social interactions,
right? A lot of social interactions in
an old feed used to be, you know, you
see some friends content, you comment on
it. Now I think the modern kind of
social interaction is you um from from a
social app is you find some content in
feed you reshare it into a group chat
and you're talking to your friends about
it there. So I I think that for all
these reasons um newsfeed and other
feeds and similar products are have kind
of evolved to be the primary thing and I
don't think that's going to change. It's
not like you know I don't think that
like we're going to get to a state where
people are using the friends tab or any
of these other things that we're going
to use necessarily more than they're
using Newsfeed. I just think that these
also have a place where they're fun and
they bring joy and can be um an
interesting thing that I think people
people want. Um and there's no reason
why they shouldn't exist in the world.
And I just think it's an interesting
quirk of the of kind of the modern
social apps and the internet that these
things that we sort of used to have um
ended up evolving into something else
and nothing really kind of like took its
place and um and and and basically you
know no one went back and built a
college service you know after you know
after Facebook went on to be a broader
thing. um you know, no one went on to I
mean, I don't want to give away too much
of the other stuff that we're doing in,
you know, phase 2, three, and four, but
but it's uh but I I I think like there's
no reason why there shouldn't be a good
friends feed of of um of content from
from from your friends. So, I think it's
going to be fun. Yeah. I don't think
it's going to be as used as like the
main news feed, but but I think people
are going to like it. And I think when
you add in a handful of other things
like this, it will change the way that
people feel about and use Facebook. Do
you feel like there's signals that
you're getting from the internet today
that suggest that maybe, you know,
people do want more of that intimate
setting, connecting with friends, or is
this a bit of like the opportunity to
reshape um the way the internet is is
moving right now? Um, I mean, it's both.
I I think people both want like really
interesting, well-made content. They
want to see the best content regardless
of whether the person they know, you
know, made it or not. you know, it could
be, you know, some creator they follow.
It could be some person who they've
never heard of. Um, so that's that's one
part of it. And then I do think people
want like real deep connections. Um, and
and you want everything in between. You
also want to be able to um, you know, I
think one of the one of the interesting
things about early social networks was
this feeling that like you could just
keep in touch with more people um, than
than you could before. And you know it
was always the case that if I wanted to
call like my best friend or you know my
mom or something I wasn't primarily
communicating with them through you know
Facebook. I wasn't like you know writing
on their wall is like the main way to
talk to them. But um but you know the
but the the kind of the superpower there
was you could have you know a few
hundred friends you could stay in touch
with people and then you know if you you
you kind of went back home or where
where you're traveling around you could
ping people who were were kind of in
that space and you could make really
interesting connections and keep in
touch with people. So I think that that
there was kind of this whole space um
the way that that gets expressed over
time changes. So the the kind of
intimate connections I think have moved
more to messaging. Yeah. And I think
that's great. Um, this whole kind of
like in between zone, there's a range of
different groups and things like that
that um that kind of enable all
different kinds of communities. Feed I
think is going to continue moving
largely in the direction of just the
best content, right? So which which I
think increasingly is created by
creators. Um, but you know, one one
dimension of best is socially relevant
to you too. Sometimes it's not just like
the funniest video. It's like the video
that tells you something about someone
who you care about or you know someone
who is relevant to your life that has
some utility to you in a different way
too. So I I think from that perspective
just having an experience where you can
make sure you get that stuff if you want
is going to be pretty valuable. I know
in the uh Q4 2024 earnings call you said
you thought Facebook could be a lot more
culturally relevant than it is. Is that
part of the reason why that you think it
was like missing some of that friendto
friend connection or you know why do you
think at least in terms of your
expectations it didn't keep pace with
that cultural relevance? I I actually
think a lot of that is around creators,
right? It's this funny dynamic where,
you know, there are more than three
billion people use Facebook and and they
don't just use it a little, right? I
mean, I think like the the average
person who's using Facebook spends more
time on it than they spend on Instagram
or a lot of other services, but um for
whatever reason, and there there are a
bunch of things that we could go
through, if you look at like who are the
kind of the top creators on the internet
today um or or even like okay, the top
thousand or 10,000, you know, they most
of them don't think of Facebook today as
their primary home for content, right?
So, a lot of people will reshare content
to Facebook and, you know, the content
becomes available there. But, and you
guys know this, it's like there's a you
know, there's when you're designing
content and and and making stuff, you
kind of have an audience and a form
factor um in mind. And, you know, so if
you're primarily doing it for Instagram,
it'll feel a certain way. If you're
primarily doing it for YouTube, it'll
feel a certain way. And then, yeah, you
might reshare it to the other places,
but like where you have in mind as your
home really matters. So I think that
today there's this kind of interesting
nature of just how it evolved because I
think Facebook did start off primarily
as friends and not as creators and and
and kind of a content feed. Um that I I
just don't think that a lot of creators
today think about Facebook as the the
primary place that they can go. But that
by itself actually creates um what is
you know if you think about it in
economic terms it's like this huge
arbitrage opportunity because all right
you have like you know three billion
people who are using this product a lot
want really good content um our
monetization systems on Facebook are
quite good um and and um and I I think
that regardless of whether you look at
it as a business opportunity for
creators or a um or just a cultural
opportunity to be able to reach a lot of
people if you invest in that um that's
something that I want to make just a lot
more attractive for creators this year.
And to me, I I think creators are
cultural relevance these days, right? I
mean, it's obviously being able to have
um connect with your friends and have
those um you know, like close
experiences. I mean, that matters a lot
too. But cultural relevance, I think the
the cultural figures that people care
about these days are creators. That's
why you have like you know, kids grow up
and you know, I think more kids aspire
to be creators than anything else. you
know, they would have maybe been, you
know, other media jobs in in in kind of
past generations, but today, this is
where the this is where kind of all the
the juice is. Did you anticipate that
when you started Facebook, or did that
surprise you that this creator economy
came about?
Um, these things always take somewhat
different shapes, but I guess like when
I was starting off, you like I I I guess
I I believed that there would be all
these different people who would want to
share and express themselves in
different ways. I primarily focused on
the social connections more than kind of
like the professional content
development. But I I think one thing
that we talked about even in the very
early days was that this was going to be
a large platform and there would be a
new set of kind of professional um like
a new professional class of people who
would create great content for this and
it was going to be different right
because um every new medium in the past
has been different right and um it
starts off kind of similar right it's
like when we had radio and then people
got TV the first people on TV were um
radio broadcasters sitting and talking
in front of a TV and then it was like no
actually you want something different
because you want people are sort of like
charismatic for TV um and can can kind
of engage in that medium. So the early
days of the internet and social media, a
lot of it was people sharing kind of
more traditional news or traditional TV,
but like that's not really optimized for
the internet. And I think now you've
kind of seen I think both because of
current events and somewhat of a loss of
trust in traditional media, but also I
think frankly just a a it's it's not
quite the engaging um format that I
think people want that that like meets
people where they are on the internet
today. So it it just makes sense that
that would sort of, you know, it's not
like these things go away. You know, we
still have radio, we're still going to
have TV, we're still, you know, all all
the all the media types that we had
before still going to exist. But I think
we're definitely entering an age where
um kind of the internet and social media
creators are the sort of most kind of
esteemed and elite cultural figures. And
I think that's really cool. It's like a
you know, people talk about wanting
generational change in leadership and
culture and this is it, right? right?
It's like now there's just this whole
set of new people who can who can kind
of um you know like make a living and
and create an influence on culture and
create different kinds of communities
and um I I don't know. I think it's
great. I think what's really interesting
is I I remember so vividly like my
brother's older than me and he he was
able to get on Facebook before me
because he was in college and I remember
the like deep desire for that exclusive
experience and when we finally got it
what played out was uh almost mirrored
real life social dynamics right
obviously it was kind of like some
people were more popular than others and
some people posted photos and got more
engagement than others. I think it's
just so interesting to me that it, you
know, you play that out all the way and
what ended up happening is like a a
shift in trust. Uh, and that that is one
of the most fascinating things. Uh, like
I I can say honestly like I remember
feeling excited if a lot of people
engaged with my content from my high
school. But to play that out into like
okay actually you can reach millions of
people and become your own uh voice.
It's Was there a moment where you
recognized like oh wow that's gonna
happen like like this is a thing that's
gonna happen? Well, well, to me, one
interesting part of this is there's a
change in sort of the whole topology of
media in that, you know, it used to be
that media was sort of organized
physically and locally, right? So, the
the um you know, there were big kind of
mainstream outlets and then um there
were local outlets. There's like local
newspapers, local TV, things like that.
And there were a handful of
interest-based kind of magazines and and
things like that.
Um, but I I I I think that the internet
has just made it so that people can
connect to the the content and the
creators who they want to engage with
way more so like that just makes this
much more natural
um growth of niches of just like all
kinds of weird and fun like interests
that um that were totally underserved
before because you know maybe there
wasn't like enough of an audience or or
a way to reach specific audience that
cared enough about a specific thing that
you could create like a whole community
and media and content around it, but on
the internet you can. And um so that to
me was some of the most interesting
stuff even early was you'd see these
groups get created or you'd see creators
start to grow who sort of represented or
talked about really kind of niche
specific interests. And I to me that's
like the beauty and the weirdness and
the greatness of the internet. I mean,
our first career was making a YouTube
channel about lacrosse. It's called the
Lacrosse Network. Um, and I actually
like largely it's not that niche. I
mean, it's a little niche. I mean, it's
niche for like, you know, the idea was
to make a TV channel about the sport and
essentially that played out across the
internet. We've been in rooms where it's
very niche where we ask people, hey,
like raise your hand and and there's not
many. But you're right, it's not that
niche. But yeah, sure. I want to say
that like you know large a lot of
calling it that had to do with uh the
movie The Social Network and just like
the excitement around that time and I'm
bringing that up because correct me if
I'm wrong but you are wearing a shirt
right now that Jesse Eisenberg wears in
the movie when Eduardo comes to the
house in Palo Alto. Is that right? Yeah,
I know that's true. I saw this online at
an auction. He's like you have to get
this. So I was like all right yeah sure
let's get it. Wait, so that's the exact
shirt? Yeah, this is his shirt. Oh my
god. Does he know it's my shirt now?
It's my shirt now. But but it was his
shirt. Yeah. Do you think he knows that
that you own the shirt now? Uh, now he
does. Now he does. Now he does. Good to
know. I mean, I've seen that movie so
many times. I'm very familiar with that
shirt. So, um, yeah. I only saw it once,
but I took our whole company to see it.
Yeah. No, I figured everyone at the
company was going to see it. So, anyway,
so we might as well just take everyone
to go see it. What's your What was your
experience watching that movie?
It was weird, man. Um, I mean, it's like
because they got all these very specific
details of like what I was wearing or
like these specific things correct, but
then like the whole narrative arc around
my motivations and all this stuff were
like completely wrong. It's like like
the the whole arc is like I'm like
somehow motivated by trying to find a
girlfriend. It's like I was dating
Priscilla before I started Facebook and
and then like you have this whole stuff
about I don't know this this is it's an
unfortunate part of the internet how
people kind of like make up a lot of the
the founding mythology to what they want
and I like it is true that I made a
bunch of other websites and stuff when I
was at Harvard but people cast it as if
like face mash that prank thing that I
made was like the precursor to Facebook.
I I guess maybe because they have face
and the name both or something. But I
mean I made a lot of stuff when I was at
Harvard. Face mash was like it was a
prank that I made, right? It was
completely separate from from Facebook.
So but yet you know to this day you know
21 years later I think largely because
of the movie people are like oh yeah you
started this as a service to rate the
attractiveness of people. It's like no I
was just a kid. I was like doing a
random different thing. It's like
Facebook is a completely different and
um and and kind of much more interesting
and ambitious project than than that.
and and and completely different. So
yeah, I don't know. And then and then
obviously the other characters in it
were like yeah, it's like all right, I
yeah, like barely knew most of them,
right? It's like it turned into this
whole thing as if this is like your the
the main uh like the the main founding
story. But anyway, yeah, makes for a
good movie. But it's uh it is
interesting now that like even even not
to be so like meta about it uh but like
we're now talking about a world where
you can you have the opportunity to just
control your own narrative like every
individual can right like whether it's
through uh podcasting content. Yeah. In
some way shape or form. Uh well you you
have the you have the ability to express
your own narrative. Um I I I think um I
mean a lot of other people also try to
to um to kind of define your narrative
for you too. So in in some cases I think
um people increasingly have an
imperative to try to define their own
narrative or else others will um
sometimes and and often nefariously
define it for you. So, um I think that
that's you know that's that's a somewhat
tricky thing but you know it um yeah I
mean it gets it gets back to
um you know we try to allow people to
express themselves in in an authentic
way and um I I think like over time you
know typically the like I I think people
have a pretty good ear for kind of
understanding what is authentic and and
what is um so what's real? So is there
like just on that note of you know OG
Facebook talking about that movie the
social network another narrative was
around like um the concept of
advertising in the in the world of um of
Facebook and you know as creators I
think a lot about the future of
advertising and and how it plays out and
I was just wondering about like thinking
through a a business case for the more
intimate maybe localized uh connections
with friends largely because I see you
know DMs being such a major part like
you said like messaging has largely gone
to DMs. I think about like, you know,
Instagram, our pieces of content. If a
piece of content hits on Instagram, it
means it has a large number of shares,
right? It has thousands of shares inside
of DMs. Is there a world where like
there there's a business case for a more
intimate version of the internet or
creators like us thinking through like
is this as we play out our business you
know is it always going to be one to
many uh advertising business or is there
actually a more localized version of
what that looks like? Yeah. No, it's
it's an interesting question. Are are
you asking from the perspective of
creators? Is there going to be a
business around messaging or are I think
I guess I'm first I'm first asking in
the context of like the platform and
like is it is it to the platform's net
benefit if there's more uh intimate
connections rather than like one creator
to many people? Well, I mean I think
when you're building these platforms you
want to build what people want, right?
So I don't I mean you know it's like the
hard part about what we do is building
services that people like. If we're able
to build a service that people like and
a billion or more people use it, we're
gonna have a fine business, right? But
our business is not driven by
advertising, right? We have a Yeah, we
have like an advertising team. It is
probably the part of the company that I
spend the least time on. The the whole
kind of differentiation of what makes us
or a company like us succeed and or or
if you're a creator is can you build
something that that people are
interested in that they want to that
they want to use that creates value and
joy in their life. And if you can do
that, then I think that there are plenty
of ways to to to basically monetize
that. And I think that that'll be true
for messaging, too. Um, but because the
kind of building the service and the
content part of it is so much harder, we
really just focus on that and getting
that to scale and then focus on building
up the business around it later. So, you
know, WhatsApp, um, you know, it has
billions of people using it. We're
really only getting to the point now um
after you know more than 10 years of of
building it here that we're really
focused on monetization around it. Um
and I think that will be I think it'll
be very interesting that that'll be kind
of the next leg of our business, right?
It's uh you know Facebook then Instagram
then WhatsApp. um that'll probably kick
in as a major kind of leg of of our
business before any of the AI stuff
reaches a massive um scale as an
independent thing or or the reality lab
stuff. So, uh but yeah, I I think that
there there will be an interesting
experience there where you can people
will be able to message with their
friends and then businesses always kind
of want to be where people are
organically. So, you know, whether
you're um from our perspective, you have
people connecting with their friends or
family or in groups. Um that's going to
create an opportunity where businesses
are going to want to engage with people
on the service that they're using. If
you're a creator, I think it's similar.
You know, we have channels and things
like that where you can engage with um
with your community. And once you you
kind of have that connection, then
whatever your business model is, whether
it's um you know, sponsored content or
if you're trying to upsell people to
other channels that you monetize and
sell subscriptions or you're trying to
sell tickets to things or merch, what
like whatever the um the you know, your
products, whatever you're making. Um I
think that that should work in in
messaging, too. It'll be a little bit
different. it'll take some some kind of
energy to figure out how to make that
work. But um but fundamentally I think
the really challenging part of it is
going to be around um kind of building
the very engaging experience. Yeah, I
think like messaging with friends is one
thing. Messaging with AI and interacting
with AI is another and that's something
that creators talk a lot about uh
specifically like AI creation. And you
mentioned there, you know, that your job
is to build things people like. I'm sure
you have to build a lot of things.
People like some things, they don't like
others. I remember we were at Meta
Connect when you launched the AI avatars
and it was, you know, Kendall Jenner or
Mr. Beast as an AI avatar. What did you
learn about people's willingness to
communicate, interact with
AI with the lens that I think creators
are sometimes
uh concerned andor skeptical, right?
Because we're looking for the real human
interaction with our content. So, we're
always curious, what have you learned
about how audiences are willing to
interact with
AI?
Um, that's interesting. So that was a
precursor to this product that we built
that we call AI studio which is
basically the ability for anyone to
craft an AI experience themselves that
both they can use if they want to use it
privately or they are going to be able
to share it and make it kind of an
experience that other people can use.
And
um I'd say you know all these things are
still pretty early and I think it's you
know there's like tens of millions of
people using it so it's a fraction of
what it will be in the future. But one
of my core beliefs on AI is that I don't
think that there's going to be like one
single AI that everyone wants to use. Um
I I think that there's going to be more
diversity and interestingness of the
content. Um
and this is sort of a different belief
that I have than that I think every
other um major company in the space has.
Right. It's like like OpenAI is building
chat GPT and Google is use is building
their thing and you know just the
different labs kind of have their main
assistant and we're building meta AI too
and a lot of people use it. It's like
okay there's 700 million people and
growing quickly who use meta AI but my
basic view is that there going to be a
lot of different things. It's not just
going to be meta AI. Like I think every
small business is going to want to have
their own AI agent um that can help out
with customer support and sales for
people who use um who who want to
interact with them. So that's hundreds
of millions of agents. I think a lot of
creators are going to want agents um
almost as sort of like a piece of
performance art but a way to also engage
with your community, right? Because a
lot of creators, you know, what we hear
is like um you know, especially if
you're a bigger creator, a lot of people
reach out to you and you want to engage
with them, but you're limited by the
amount of hours in the day and they want
to have some engagement with you, but um
